title: REMAR Project Launched: €5.5M to Restore Eastern Caribbean Mangroves
detail: The Resilient Ecosystems through Mangrove Restoration (REMAR) project was virtually launched on April 11, 2026, a €5.5 million, five-year initiative to restore and protect mangrove ecosystems across five Caribbean territories: Grenada, Saint Lucia, Saint Vincent and the Grenadines, Martinique, and Guadeloupe. Led by the Organisation of Eastern Caribbean States (OECS) Commission in partnership with France's Agence Française de Développement (AFD) and the French Facility for Global Environment (FFEM), REMAR operates on three pillars: community-based restoration, scientific knowledge enhancement, and institutional capacity building. The initiative addresses compounding threats from climate change, intensifying hurricanes, and sargassum seaweed inundation that have accelerated mangrove loss across the Eastern Caribbean over the past decade.

media.caption: REMAR project launch for Caribbean mangrove restoration across five OECS territories

title: NPR Investigation: Great Green Wall Only 13% of $19B Pledged Disbursed
detail: An in-depth NPR investigative assessment published April 11, 2026 found that 18 years into the Great Green Wall initiative, most of the planned 4,350-mile belt across Africa 'remains no more green than barren fields.' The investigation found that only 13% of the $19 billion pledged for the GGW had actually been disbursed as of 2023 — approximately $2.5 billion of the total. Key challenges cited include poorly planned projects that lacked local community participation, opaque financing mechanisms, and inadequate coordination among the 11 Sahel nations in the corridor. The estimated cost to complete the GGW is $33 billion. The assessment noted cautious optimism tied to new Pan-African Agency leadership taking a more community-centred approach, but described current trajectory as far below what is needed to meet the 2030 target.

media.caption: NPR investigative report on Great Green Wall progress and funding disbursement gaps
